title: Rate limiting per API key and per tenant using ETS counters

story.i_want_to: implement rate limiting using ETS counters that enforces per-API-key and per-tenant request limits, configurable via tenant settings, with standard rate limit headers in responses
story.so_that: the system is protected from abuse, runaway agents, and resource exhaustion, with per-tenant configurability and clear signaling to clients when limits are approached or exceeded

rationale: Without rate limiting, a single misbehaving agent could overwhelm the system and degrade service for all tenants. ETS-based counters are fast (in-memory, no external dependency), suitable for a single-node deployment, and reset naturally with application restarts. Per-tenant configuration allows premium tenants to have higher limits. Rate limit headers follow the IETF standard so agents can self-throttle.

acceptance_criteria.description: A LoopctlWeb.Plugs.RateLimiter plug tracks request counts per API key per minute using an ETS table
The default rate limit is 300 requests per minute per API key (configurable via tenant settings rate_limit_requests_per_minute)
When the rate limit is exceeded, the plug halts the connection with 429 Too Many Requests and a JSON error body
Every response includes rate limit headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp of window reset)
A per-tenant aggregate limit exists (sum of all API key requests) that is 3x the per-key limit by default
Superadmin keys are exempt from rate limiting
The ETS table is owned by a GenServer that handles periodic cleanup of expired window counters (every minute)
Rate limit counters use :ets.update_counter for atomic, lock-free increments

test_cases.name: Request within rate limit succeeds with headers
expected_results: Response has X-RateLimit-Reset header with a future Unix timestamp

test_cases.name: Request exceeding rate limit returns 429
expected_results: 4th request returns 429 with JSON error body

test_cases.name: Rate limit resets after window expires
steps: Simulate window expiration (manipulate ETS timestamps or mock time)
expected_results: After window reset, the request succeeds

test_cases.name: Superadmin key bypasses rate limiting
steps: Send many requests rapidly with the superadmin key
expected_results: No rate limit headers are present (or they show unlimited)

test_cases.name: Tenant-level custom rate limit is respected
ETS cleanup removes expired windows
steps: Insert expired window entries into the ETS table

technical_notes: ETS table options: :set, :public, read_concurrency: true, write_concurrency: true
Key format in ETS: {api_key_id, window_timestamp} -> count
:ets.update_counter(table, key, {2, 1}, {key, 0}) for atomic increment with default
GenServer sends itself a periodic message every 60 seconds to clean expired windows
The RateLimiter plug should be the LAST plug in the auth pipeline (after RequireAuth), so unauthenticated requests don't consume rate limit budget
Consider using :atomics instead of ETS for even better performance (Erlang/OTP 21+)
Test note: rate limit tests may need to reset ETS state between tests to avoid pollution
